coredata.dc:title: Covalent organic framework incorporated outer-selective hollow fiber thin-film nanocomposite membranes for osmotically driven desalination
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Sungil
Akther, Nawshad
Tran, Van Huy
Bae, Tae-Hyun
Phuntsho, Sherub
Merenda, Andrea
Dumée, Ludovic F.
Shon, Ho Kyong
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, new outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) thin film nanocomposite (TFN) forward osmosis (FO) membranes incorporated with amine-rich Schiff-based network (SNW-1) nanoparticles (NPs), a type of covalent organic frameworks (COFs), were developed by vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP). The SNW-1 NPs possess hydrophilic nature and porous internal structures, which are desirable for producing highly permeable and efficient FO membranes. SNW-1 NPs were conformally packed across the outer surface of the HF substrate by vacuum pressure applied during the VAIP process leading to defect-free coatings. Furthermore, covalent bonding between secondary amine (NH2 −) groups across the SNW-1 network and remanent carboxylic groups present from the monomer used for the interfacial polymerization step supported the adhesion of SNW-1 NPs to the native poly(amide) layer present across the TFN membrane surface. As a result, SNW-1 loading at 0.001 wt% exhibited the best FO performance with enhanced water flux more than 23% of the pristine one (31.5 L m−2 h−1) and relatively low specific reverse solute flux (SRSF, J
s /J
w ) at 0.18 g L−1 using 1 M NaCl and DI water in series of TFN composite membranes. The optimum loading at 0.001 wt% was much lower than those in other home-made TFN membranes (normally above 0.05 wt%) due to the minimum loss of SNW-1 in the VAIP. Also, the TFN membrane offered excellent FO operation stability tested for 72 h. Such novel approach is promising to optimize FO processes for desalination and water treatment in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Forward osmosis
Outer-selective hollow fiber
Schiff-based network
Thin-film nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of hollow fiber membrane substrates
Preparation of OSHF TFN membranes using SNW-1
Characterization of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1
FO performance of OSHF TFN membranes
Characterizations of SNW-1 NPs for fabricating OSHF TFN membranes
Characterizations of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1 NPs
FO performance of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1 NPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Environmental Industry and Technology Institute

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of expected phenomena about positioning SNW-1 inside PA layer under (a) general IP, and (b) the VAIP process, and expected chemical reaction with secondary amine groups in SNW-1 and acyl chloride groups in the TMC solution through the amide-based covalent bonding.

Surface images of MPD- (S0s) and (b) MPD/SNW-1 (S3s, S5s, S10s and S15s) soaked membrane substrates after conducting the vacuum suction in the VAIP process, and high-magnified image of aggregated SNW-1 NPs in S15s.

Morphological SEM images (×3000) of the PA selective layer of TFC (S0) and TFN (S3, 5, 10, and 15) membrane samples (a), and high-magnification images (×50,000) of particle-free S0 (left) and S15 with observing SNW-1 NPs (right) inside the PA layer (b).
AFM images and average roughness of selected membrane samples according to SNW-1loading (a), and comparison of the contact angle of OSHF TFC and TFN membranes using SNW-1 nanoparticles (b).
IR spectrums (left: 800–1800 cm−1 and right: 1200–1700 cm−1) of OSHF TFC (S0) and TFN membranes using SNW-1 nanoparticles (S3, S5, S10 and S15).

Comparison of key FO performances (water flux — bar, and SRSF — red dot) of OSHF TFC (S0) and TFN membranes according to different SNW-1 loading from 0 to 0.015 wt%. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Water flux and (b) SRSF profiles of S0 (TFC) and S10 (TFN) OSHF membranes according to different concentration of DS from 0.5 to 2 M.

Water flux of the best membrane candidate (S10) for 72 h in order to evaluate the membrane's stability. The DS and FS was 1 M NaCl solution and DI water, respectively. These solutions were refreshed every 4 h in the operation for 72 h in order to maintain the osmotic driving force.
Chemical compositions of the VAIP process for OSHF membrane samples.
Intrinsic transport properties of selected OSHF TFN FO membranes (estimated by the model calculation using the FO performance presented in Fig. 7).

head.ce:title: Covalent organic framework incorporated outer-selective hollow fiber thin-film nanocomposite membranes for osmotically driven desalination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater, School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Technology Sydney (UTS), Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater
School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater, School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Technology Sydney (UTS), Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Deakin University, Geelong Institute for Frontier Materials, Waurn Ponds, 3216, Victoria, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Geelong Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, new outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) thin film nanocomposite (TFN) forward osmosis (FO) membranes incorporated with amine-rich Schiff-based network (SNW-1) nanoparticles (NPs), a type of covalent organic frameworks (COFs), were developed by vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP). The SNW-1 NPs possess hydrophilic nature and porous internal structures, which are desirable for producing highly permeable and efficient FO membranes. SNW-1 NPs were conformally packed across the outer surface of the HF substrate by vacuum pressure applied during the VAIP process leading to defect-free coatings. Furthermore, covalent bonding between secondary amine (NH2 −) groups across the SNW-1 network and remanent carboxylic groups present from the monomer used for the interfacial polymerization step supported the adhesion of SNW-1 NPs to the native poly(amide) layer present across the TFN membrane surface. As a result, SNW-1 loading at 0.001 wt% exhibited the best FO performance with enhanced water flux more than 23% of the pristine one (31.5 L m−2 h−1) and relatively low specific reverse solute flux (SRSF, J
s /J
w ) at 0.18 g L−1 using 1 M NaCl and DI water in series of TFN composite membranes. The optimum loading at 0.001 wt% was much lower than those in other home-made TFN membranes (normally above 0.05 wt%) due to the minimum loss of SNW-1 in the VAIP. Also, the TFN membrane offered excellent FO operation stability tested for 72 h. Such novel approach is promising to optimize FO processes for desalination and water treatment in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: FO membrane processes have been recently vitalized for low-energy desalination [1–4], wastewater treatment [5–7], food processing [8] and energy harvesting [9,10]. Unlike pressure-driven membrane processes for water treatment such as microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO) filtration, the driving force of FO processes is naturally produced by the osmotic pressure between high saline water and low saline feed water contained with impurities across a semi-permeable membrane. Since there is no requirement for applied hydraulic pressure under FO operation, advantages of the FO processes for water treatment and desalination include lower energy consumption and less fouling potential than conventional RO processes. In addition, various FO hybrid applications have been investigated such as FO-RO hybrid process for low-energy desalination, osmotic membrane bioreactor (OMBR) with aerobic or anaerobic digestions for wastewater treatment, fertilizer-drawn FO (FDFO) processes for fertilizer irrigation in agricultural area [2,11–13].

Although FO represents an emerging eco-friendly membrane technology for desalination or other water treatment processes, there are existing obstacles to optimize and demonstrate large-scale FO applications. One major challenge is related to identifying the suitable draw solution for energy- and cost-effective regeneration [14–17]. The second challenge is related to the design of the membrane materials requiring to be further tune to match FO requirements. FO membranes should be highly permeable to water, strictly ion selective, mechanically stable and provide anti-fouling performance to enhance the membrane durability and cost-effectiveness of the process [18–20]. Based on these requirements, recently developed and commercial FO membranes were mainly developed as thin-film composite (TFC) membranes, which consist of a thin polyamide (PA) active layer, a porous membrane substrate and backing fabric. The main advantage of TFC membranes is that each layer may be individually customized to improve membrane performance. Performance during FO process relies on both the mitigation of internal concentration polarization (ICP) and the control of water transport across the surface and free volume of the TFC membranes respectively [21–23]. Although the selective outer layer plays an important role over the operation, the development of highly permeable and ion selective TFC FO membranes with higher water and solute permeabilities is challenging the trade-off between water flux and reverse solute diffusion [23–26].

New strategies to alter the intrinsic transport properties of FO membranes for enhancing membrane performance were investigated [27–29]. Several studies demonstrated the modification of membrane substrate or PA active layer by incorporating various hydrophilic nanomaterials such as graphene oxide and silica [30–33]. Representative hydrophilic nanofillers including carbon-based nanomaterials such as functionalized graphene, graphene oxide [34–37], functionalized carbon nanotubes [38,39] and carbon quantum dots [40,41], inorganic nanomaterials such as silica, zeolite and halloysite nanotubes [42,43], and metal-based nanoparticles (NPs) like Ag and TiO2 [29] have been considered as nanofillers to incorporate into a membrane substrate for mixed matrix membranes or into a PA selective layer for thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) FO membranes [24].

In addition, various kinds of framework-based materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) has been investigated as a new category of nanofillers for developing nanocomposite membranes [23,31,44,45]. COFs especially possess highly microporous structures, tuneable chemical functionalities and an ability for selective grafting with various chemistries due to the organic linkers on the building blocks [23,31,44]. These unique characteristics of COFs may contribute to customize the physicochemical properties of polymeric membranes to be highly water permeable due to their porous structures and hydrophilicity. Our previous study presented that the flat-sheet TFN membranes incorporated with amine-rich Schiff-based network-1 (SNW-1) COFs into PA layer were recently developed for FO applications [46]. That study mentioned that the porous SNW-1 NPs inside PA layer produced more pathways for water transport from its porous morphology, higher hydrophilicity from high density of amine-based functional groups and better compatibility with a thin PA layer due to covalent bonding with the acyl chloride in interfacial polymerization (IP) processes. As a novel nanofiller, COFs including SNW-1 may be used to fabricate a highly permeable polymeric membrane, when the COFs are located inside a thin PA layer by their own characteristics.

In our previous studies, successful development of outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) TFC membranes for FO applications was reported, exhibiting specific advantages for FO applications [47–49]. Furthermore, COF SNW-1 porous NPs were effectively used to produce high-permeable TFN FO membranes from literature [46]. To the best of our knowledge, we for the first time aim to successfully develop outstanding OSHF TFN membranes using the SNW-1 NPs for enhancing water permeability with stable integrity of the PA/SNW-1 composite layer. A new hypothesis of conceptual methodologies and expected formation mechanisms incorporating SNW-1 into the PA active layer of OSHF TFN membranes was proposed. Then, the OSHF TFN membranes were subsequently designed and fabricated, following the proposed hypothesis. We finally evaluated the performance of nascent OSHF TFN membranes using lab-scale tests on key FO performance parameters and membrane stability over long-term experiments.

For preparing HF membrane substrates, poly(ethersulfone) powder (PES, Veradel® 3000P, Solvay) with the molecular weight at 63,000 g/mol and 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, Merck) as a solvent of PES were used. As an additive for altering pore formation, poly(ethylene glycol) 400 (PEG400, Sigma-Aldrich) at 5 wt% was mixed into the polymer solution. For conducting interfacial polymerization to form a PA layer on the membrane substrate, amine-based 1,2-phenylenediamine (MPD, 99%, Sigma-Aldrich) aqueous solution and chlorine-based trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) in organic solution was used. For long-term storage of HF membranes, membrane substrates were immersed into the glycerol-based aqueous solution (50/50 wt%) for 24 h and subsequently dried in air in order to reserve surface and internal morphology of HF membrane substrates. As a nanofiller for TFN membranes, home-made COF as denoted as SNW-1 was synthesized with melamine (99%, Macllin Reagent), terephthalaldehyde (98%, Macllin Reagent) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, analytical grade, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent) as key materials for its synthesis and dichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran (THF) and acetone manufactured by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent for the post-treatment of SNW-1. For FO performance tests, deionized water with the resistivity at 18 MΩ/cm (Milli-Q, Millipore) and sodium chloride (Chem Supply) were employed as FS and draw solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hollow fiber membrane substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The spinning parameters to fabricate the HF membrane substrates for preparing OSHF TFC membranes were optimized in our previous study [48]. The dry-jet wet spinneret machine was used to cast HF membrane substrates. Dried PES powder was dissolved in NMP solution at 16.5 wt% with an additive of PEG400 at 5 wt% for 12 h using the temperature-controlled magnetic stirrer (AREC, VELP Scientifica). The prepared dope solution was pre-filtered by fabric mesh to remove undesirable impurities and bubbles. After that, the prepared dope solution was placed in the syringe pump (Model 500D, Teledyne ISCO) and left overnight to degas. In the membrane casting, the specific spinning parameters were confirmed and tabulated in Table S1 as what we optimized to prepare HF membrane substrates for subsequent polyamide (PA) active layer deposition via the VAIP method in our previous study [48]. Prepared HF membrane substrates were stored in DI water for 2 days to remove solvents occupied in polymeric layers fully, and then immersed in glycerin-based aqueous solution for 12 h and dried in the atmosphere for their long-term preservation.

As aforementioned, for installing laboratory-scale membrane modules, two fibers of membrane substrates in the length of 13.5 cm were prepared and carefully spaced inside customized acryl module for subsequent FO performance tests. Each edge of membrane fibers in the modules were capped by potting with a general epoxy resin with retaining their bore holes at each side. The effective surface area was estimated at 6.5 cm2 per each module.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OSHF TFN membranes using SNW-1
ce:section.ce:para: As the main nanofiller, the COF NPs denoted as SNW-1 was synthesized by using the solvothermal method as described elsewhere [46,50]. For preparing OSHF TFN membranes, our modified vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP) was applied with amine-based MPD aqueous solution and chlorine-based TMC organic solution. The detailed methodological procedure of the modified VAIP for OSHF TFC membranes was described in detail in our paper [51]. To prepare these solutions, the SNW-1 powder was suspended in DI water and sonicated by the laboratory-scale sonicator for over 6 h. The loading of SNW-1 in DI water was varied in the range of 0 to 20 mg L−1, and then the MPD flake at 2 wt% and SDS powder at 0.2 wt% were added and mixed into the SNW-1 spiked DI water by a magnetic stirrer under room atmosphere. After the VAIP process, the prepared OSHF TFN membrane was washed by DI water several times and subsequently stored in DI water at 4 °C for its future testing. In this study, the SNW-1 loading was varied from 0 to 0.002 wt%, and the prepared OSHF TFN membranes were denoted as S# according to SNW-1 loading as shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1
ce:section.ce:para: For observing surface and internal morphologies of membrane samples, the field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Carl Zeiss AG) was mainly used with an accelerating voltage of 5–10 kV. Before conducting the SEM analysis, the specimens were placed on the aluminium stubs, and then they were fully dried by N2 compressed gas. Subsequently, the samples were coated with a 10-nm gold‑palladium (AuPd) layer with a metal sputtering machine (EM ACE600, Leica). In addition, for characterizing chemical changes of membrane samples, a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Affinity-1, Shimadzu) was used under the signal range of 800 to 2000 cm−1 (signal resolution at 1 cm−1 and minimum 16 scans). The surface topography was analyzed by the atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park XE-7, Park Systems) under the atmosphere tapping and scanning at 5 to 5 μm, and the image software was used to determine the mean roughness of the samples. Moreover, the membrane's hydrophilicity was confirmed by using the sessile-drop optical subsystem (Theta Lite 100, Biolin Scientific) including its image-analysis program.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FO performance of OSHF TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The FO performance test was conducted to evaluate intrinsic FO performance of home-made OSHF TFN membranes using the lab-scale FO unit described elsewhere [36,48]. For the test, HF membrane modules including a series of TFN FO membranes were prepared and assembled into the FO test unit. The membrane orientation under the testing operation was AL-FS (FO mode) which means that FS is facing to the active layer in the module. The driving force of the FO operation was produced by the osmotic pressure between 1 M NaCl solution as DS and deionized water as FS. The cross-flow velocity of feed and draw stream was 20.9 cm/s and 10.4 cm/s under co-current flow mode, respectively. Temperatures of both FS and DS were stably controlled at 23 ± 0.5 °C by using the temperature controller.

The long-term operating test for 3 days was conducted to offer a more reliable and practical evaluation of the membrane's performance and stability. In this test, the best membrane candidate in the FO performance test was selected to conduct the stability test. The selected FO membrane was operated under the typical FO operation over 72 h, and the feed and draw solution were refreshed every 12 h in order to minimize the osmotic dilution in the FO process. The water flux and RSF of the membrane samples in the stability test were continuously monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of SNW-1 NPs for fabricating OSHF TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: As the key nanofiller, the COF based SNW-1 NPs were used for preparing the OSHF TFN membranes in this study. The produced SNW-1 NPs were observed as a uniform and smooth spherical shape in the size range of 20–40 nm as presented in Fig. 1 . The Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) surface area, Non-Localized Density Functional Theory (NL-DFT) volume and mean pore size of the SNW-1 NPs were 500 m2 g−1 and 0.14 cm3 g−1 and 5.5 Å, respectively [46]. Another study also presented similar physical properties of their home-made SNW-1 NPs with our one [44]. The SNW-1 NPs are made of melamine-based porous organic-based frameworks, and Gao et al. and Akther et al. reported that the average internal pore size of SNW-1 NPs was calculated at around 5 Å [46,53]. As shown in Fig. S1(a), for TFN membranes with SNW-1, the porous internal structures of SNW-1 may provide sub-1 nm extra water channels inside a dense PA layer [44,46,53]. In addition, small and smooth external shape of SNW-1 NPs (Fig. S1(b)) may contribute to embedding the NPs into a thin PA layer with less deterioration of the layer's integrity. The SNW-1 NPs were well dispersed in DI water due to the existence of the secondary amine group on the SNW-1's surface. This is because SNW-1 NPs possess strong affinity for water molecules presented in Fig. S1(c), which can be a preferable property for developing TFN membranes [46].

In typical IP processes for preparing TFN membranes, hydrophilic nanofillers are normally blended with the MPD solution, and the mixed MPD solution is initially immersed on a membrane substrate. After allowing for specific time for absorbing the solution into surface pores, excess MPD solution on the surface is generally removed by nitrogen flushing or rubber roller. At that time, it is expected that excess nanofillers across the membrane surface may be removed so that the effective nanofillers loading may be accurately performed. The expected phenomenon is illustrated in Fig. 1. Unlike the general IP process, the excess MPD solution on the surface of a HF substrate is removed by suction to inside the HF substrate at the specific vacuum pressure in the VAIP process. At the same time, loosely settled SNW-1 NPs across the surface can be strongly accumulated by the vacuum pressure onto the bottom as illustrated in Fig. 1(a) so that most SNW-1 NPs could be placed on the surface with less particle loss. Subsequently, the stacked SNW-1 NPs on the surface and MPD solution inside the pores would be reacted with the organic TMC solution, and at the same time the deposited SNW-1 NPs would be properly embedded into the PA layer. For supporting the hypothesis, we found that optimum concentrations of nanofillers in other related studies were mainly over 0.05 wt% under the general IP, which is over 50 times more than the optimum SNW-1 loading (0.001 wt%) under the VAIP in this study [33]. Thus, employing VAIP technique can contribute towards reducing the production cost of TFN FO membranes by minimizing nanoparticle loss during the fabrication process. Furthermore, as presented in Fig. 1(c), the secondary amine groups surrounding the SNW-1 may react with the acyl chloride groups in TMC solution under the IP process, and the strong covalent bonds would be subsequently formed between SNW-1 and TMC solution [44]. The formed amide-based thin layer on the SNW-1 may positively affect the integrity of the PA composite layer with minimizing the voids or defects from SNW-1. It thus hypothesizes that the SNW-1 NPs might be properly incorporated into a thin PA layer in the VAIP based on the physic-chemical expectations presented in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the surface images of the membrane substrates soaked with the MPD solution (denoted as S0s) and MPD/SNW-1 (denoted as S3s, S5s, S10s and S15s) blended solution after conducting the vacuum suction in the VAIP. S0s presented that there is no particulate on the surface of the MPD-soaked membrane substrate as expected. However, Fig. 3 shows that NPs clusters are visible across the surfaces of the MPD/SNW-1 soaked substrates. When the NPs' loadings are increased from S3s to S15s, the number and size of the NPs clusters are proportionally increased due to NPs' aggregation. As shown in high magnified SEM images in Fig. 2, the NPs clusters were made of SNW-1 NPs in the size at around 40 nm which is similar with that presented in Fig. 2(b). Although sub-50 nm SNW-1 particles were not distributed as a single-particle across the surface with the evidence of their aggregation, bunches of partially aggregated SNW-1 NPs were appropriately accumulated and distributed onto the surface prior to reacting with the TMC solution. However, S15s presented an aggregated NPs' dump at the size over 5 μm because of exceed loading of SNW-1 NPs, and this may subsequently deteriorate integrity of a PA layer. Although the SNW-1 bunches might be properly settled on the surface under the condition, the concentration of SNW-1 NPs may sensitively affect the quality of the PA composite layer. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the dosing ratio of SNW-1 for fabricating suitable TFN membranes.

Fig. 3 presents morphological images of the PA selective layers of all series of FO membranes. In these images, S0 membrane presented typical ridge-and-valley morphologies of the PA layer what many groups have reported [36,54,55]. With the addition of SNW-1 into a PA layer, S3 and S5 membranes demonstrate almost similar PA structures with that of neat TFC membrane (S0). The S10 and S15 membranes, on the other hand, showed bulged spots across the surface of the PA layers, but the other sides presented quite flat and smooth surface morphology. It means that the PA/SNW-1 composite layer might be aggregated by the higher SNW-1 NPs' loading in the VAIP, since SNW-1 NPs in MPD solution might interfere the chemical reaction with the MPD and TMC solution in IP process [56,57]. Furthermore, the high-magnified SEM image of S15 in Fig. 3(b) showed a lot of spherically bulged spots on the PA structure indicated by the yellow arrows compared to that of S0 (Fig. 3(a)), which might be an evidence of the NPs incorporation or chemical interference. Therefore, it is noteworthy to mention that excess NPs loading is able to cause deforming typical PA's ridge-and-valley structures, which may subsequently deteriorate membrane's selectivity.

Using the AFM analysis, the 3D images and mean roughness (Ra) of the surfaces of selected OSHF TFN membranes (S0, S10 and S15) were produced as shown in Fig. 4(a). For particle-free S0 membrane, the ridge-valley-structure was shown in 3D image, and its Ra was 107.2 nm. In comparison of AFM results, it confirmed that the TFC membrane possesses the smoothest active surface, and Ra values of the TFN membranes were proportionally varied from 107.2 nm of S0 to 135.6 nm of S15 with an increase of SNW-1 loading. Higher roughness from the increase of SNW-1 loading may beneficially contribute to increasing membrane's surface area, however, it is required to optimize nanofiller's loading not to damage the thin PA layer by the incorporated fillers. Hydrophilicity of membrane's active layer was examined to investigate the water affinity of the PA composite layer incorporated with SNW-1 NPs. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the contact angle values of SNW-1 incorporated membranes (S3, 5, 10 and 15) below 60° were lower than that of pristine one (S0) at 73.8°, since SNW-1 NPs possess hydrophilic amine-rich groups on their surface. However, with an increase of SNW-1 loading, the contact angles were slightly increased from 51° to 56°, although loading of the hydrophilic SNW-1 NPs was increased. As shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4(a), the rough morphology on the surface of the SNW-1 composite PA layer might result from the protrusion of SNW-1 NPs, and it may cause to increase contact angle of water droplets because of trapped air pockets in rough valleys [37,58]. Therefore, in the increase of SNW-1 loading into a PA layer, the composite PA layer would be altered to be rougher and hydrophilic due to its morphological structure and chemical functionality which may be beneficial for enhancing membrane's water permeability [46].

The chemical analysis on the surface of the TFC and TFN membranes was conducted using FT-IR in order to investigate the existence of SNW-1 NPs and chemical changes of nanocomposite PA layer with SNW-1. Fig. 5 presents IR spectrums of TFC and TFN membranes incorporated with SNW-1 NPs. The IR spectrums of the TFC membrane clearly presented some representative peaks of a PES substrate at 1150 cm−1 for CSO2 C stretch and at 1240 cm−1 for COC stretch and a thin PA layer between 1505 and 1540 cm−1 for amide II (CN stretch), 1610 cm−1 for the aromatic ring from the amide bonding and between 1650 and 1665 cm−1 for amide I [46]. Therefore, these peaks were considered to validate the PA layer of the OSHF TFC membranes. For TFN membranes with SNW-1, the intensity of the amide II's peak at 1540 cm−1 was proportionally increased when the SNW-1 loading was increased due to the increment of amide bonding from SNW-1. Furthermore, the specific peaks at 1460 and 1535 cm−1 were distinguished in the TFN membranes with higher loading of SNW-1, which is identified as triazine rings from SNW-1 [46]. In addition, the IR peaks at 1480 cm−1 presented in only TFN membranes represent CN vibration of the semicircle stretching of the triazine ring from SNW-1. These IR results verify that the SNW-1 NPs were successfully incorporated into the thin PA layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FO performance of OSHF TFN membranes with SNW-1 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The key FO performance of the TFC and TFN membranes were determined and evaluated under the FO operation (AL-FS orientation) using the lab-scale FO unit. The water flux and SRSF of series of the TFC (S0) and TFN (S3, 5, 10 and 15) membranes are shown in Fig. 6 . The neat TFC membrane (S0) exhibited the water flux at 25.5 L m−2 h−1 and SRSF at 0.21 g L−1. For TFN SNW-1 membranes, the water flux was gradually increased with no sacrifice of the SRSF, when the particle loading was increased up to 0.001 wt%. At 0.001 wt%, S10 presented the highest water flux at 31.5 L m−2 h−1 (Improved 24% of the water flux of S0) with the relatively low SRSF at 0.18 g L−1 in series of TFN membranes. This result implies that the SNW-1 particles inside PA layer may contribute to modifying intrinsic transport property of the PA layer due to their unique characteristics such as porous frameworks themselves and hydrophilic nature, which are preferable for enhancing water transport. In addition, the SNW-1 might contribute to increase the free volume of the PA composite layer due to the incompatibility with a PA polymeric structure. However, increasing the SNW-1 loading beyond the optimal value at 0.001 wt% deteriorated the FO performance of the OSHF TFN membranes. In particular, the water flux of S15 declined to 22.6 L m−2 h−1, and its SRSF increased to 0.41 g L−1. The overloading of SNW-1 NPs inside a PA layer formed a defective the PA layer, which reduced membrane selectivity. Consequently, the osmotic driving force across the membrane decreased, which resulted in lower water flux. Therefore, it can be established based on the FO performance that the optimal SNW-1 loading for preparing the OSHF TFN membrane is 0.001 wt%.

The comparative profiles of water flux and SRSF of the particle-free OSHF TFC (S0) and the best OSHF TFN membrane (S10) as a function of NaCl concentration from 0.5 to 2 M in a draw stream were determined in Fig. 7 . The water fluxes of these membranes were proportionally increased due to a constant increase of the osmotic pressure as their driving force. However, the trends of SRSF results were stably maintained regardless of the difference of the driving force, as the trade-off relationship between water permeability and ion selectivity was retained even though the driving force was changed in FO operation. In the comparison, all water fluxes of S10 in all range of DS concentrations were higher than those of S0 due to higher water permeability of S10 as expected. As a result, S10 exhibited the highest water flux of 42.8 L m−2 h−1 with a stable SRSF at 0.22 g L−1 using 2 M NaCl solution as DS.

Using the profiles in Fig. 7, the intrinsic transport properties of the OSHF membranes (S0 and S10) were estimated by the FO model algorithm developed by Tiraferri and his colleagues [52]. Table 2 presents calculated water permeability (A), solute permeability coefficient (B), intrinsic selectivity (B/A) and structural parameter (S) of the OSHF TFC (S0) and the best OSHF TFN (S10) membranes. The A value of S10 at 2.37 L−1 m−2 h−1 bar−1 was higher than that of S0 at 1.68 L−1 m−2 h−1 bar−1. However, the intrinsic selectivity of S10 at 0.18 bar was almost similar with that of S0 at 0.17 bar, which implies that the water permeability of S10 was improved with retaining ion selectivity compared to those of S0. The S value of S10 was similar with that of S0 due to the use of the same membrane substrates.

Most TFN membranes have been concerned about degrade of the PA selective layer over the operation time due to delamination of NPs from the thin PA layer. The long-term performance test of the best OSHF TFN membrane was thus conducted in order to further investigate the membrane's stability under FO operation. For TFN membranes, it is necessary to clearly show the FO performance in long-term operation for evaluating the membrane's stability, due to the risk of releasing nanofillers from a PA composite layer under the crossflow stream in FO operation, which may partially damage the PA active layer. Fig. 8 presents the water flux of S10 in the long-term operation for 72 h. The S10 exhibited a remarkably stable water flux for the period. This result shows that incorporated nanofillers (SNW-1) in S10 were strongly linked with the PA matrix so that there is no concern about the deterioration of the FO performance under a long-term operation. Therefore, the optimum membrane (S10) is able to offer great potential for practical FO applications.

One COF material, SNW-1 was successfully applied to the development of novel OSHF TFN membranes for FO applications. In particular, the SNW-1 NPs were properly incorporated into the PA layer under the VAIP process with performance of the TFN membranes significantly improved with no detrimental issue of ion selectivity. The expected mechanisms for incorporating the SNW-1 NPs into PA layer in our hypothesis were successfully demonstrated based on the analysis and experimental results. The SNW-1 NPs were stacked and accumulated on the outer surface of HF substrates with minimum loss of particles by the vacuum pressure. Furthermore, the secondary amine groups in SNW-1 and acyl chloride groups in the TMC solution might chemically react to form an amide layer via the covalent bonding. These expected phenomena might strongly support to form a defect-free PA nanocomposite layer with SNW-1. The SNW-1 loading was manipulated and optimized in the extremely low range from 0 to 0.0015 wt%, as such S10 sample (SNW-1 at 0.001 wt%) exhibited the best FO performance with the highest water flux at 31.5 L m−2 h−1 and reasonable SRSF at 0.18 g L−1 in series of TFN membranes. The enhanced FO performance was attributed to the unique nature of SNW-1 itself like highly porous framework morphology and amine-based hydrophilic property, and good integrity of produced PA composite layer with no sacrifice of ion selectivity. The optimum TFN membrane (S10) exhibited high water flux at around 30 L m−2 h−1 and excellent stability of the FO performance with no decline of the water flux in the long-term operation for 72 h. Through the long-term experiment, it is noteworthy to mention that the optimum TFN membrane (S10) demonstrate the strong durability for its commercial application. These findings prove that the optimum sample (S10) can be a promising candidate to produce highly permeable FO performances for desalination and water treatment.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Sungil Lim:Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Nawshad Akther:Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Van Huy Tran:Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Tae-Hyun Bae:Investigation, Methodology. Sherub Phuntsho:Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Validation, Visualization. Andrea Merenda:Writing - review & editing, Validation, Visualization. Ludovic F. Dumée:Writing - review & editing, Validation, Visualization. Ho Kyong Shon:Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a grant from the Australia Research Council (ARC) Future Fellowship (FT140101208) and Discovery Early Career Researcher Award (DECRA, DE180100130). This work was supported by Korea Environment Industry & Technology Institute (KEITI) through Industrial Facilities & Infrastructure Research Program, funded by Korea Ministry of Environment (MOE) (1485016285).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2020.114461.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Choice of apposite dispersing medium for silica nanoparticles leading to their effective embedment in nanocomposite nanofiltration membranes
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Superhydrophobic nanofiber membrane containing carbon nanotubes for high-performance direct contact membrane distillation
